text.title: django-decision-tree Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for django-decision-tree. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use Django when: building a web application (not CLI-only), needing an ORM, requiring an admin interface, managing user accounts and permissions, handling complex forms, or requiring server-side rendering. Consider FastAPI when: building API-only services, prioritizing async-first design, needing minimal dependencies, or the team prefers the FastAPI ecosystem.
rule.rationale: Django's batteries-included approach makes full-featured web applications quick to prototype but adds overhead for minimal APIs. FastAPI excels at async-first, type-hinted APIs but requires manual setup for admin, auth, and ORM integration. Decision should be based on specific project requirements rather than general assumptions.

rule.statement: Use DRF when: large existing DRF codebase, needing ViewSets for CRUD, requiring extensive serializer ecosystem, or prioritizing community resources. Use Django Ninja when: starting new project, prioritizing performance and type hints, preferring FastAPI-like DX, or needing auto-generated OpenAPI docs without extra packages.
rule.rationale: DRF dominates market share and has mature third-party packages (drf-spectacular, rest-framework-simplejwt). Django Ninja is newer and lighter, with faster performance due to reduced serialization overhead and native async support. Choose based on project context, team experience, and specific requirements.

rule.statement: Use Simple (Fat Models) for: small projects (less than 5 apps, less than 10 models), MVPs, prototypes, or 1-2 person teams. Use Service Layer for: medium projects (5-15 apps, 10-50 models), growing teams, multiple entry points, and moderate business logic. Use Clean Architecture / DDD for: large enterprise projects (greater than 15 apps, greater than 50 models), long-term maintenance, complex domain logic, or multiple bounded contexts.
rule.rationale: Simple architecture reduces boilerplate for small projects but becomes unmaintainable as complexity grows. Service Layer keeps business logic testable and reusable. Clean Architecture enforces isolation for large teams. Selection depends on project scope, team size, and maintenance horizon.

rule.statement: Use PostgreSQL for: production environments, complex queries (CTEs, window functions), full-text search, JSON/JSONB fields, geographic data (PostGIS), or high write concurrency. Use SQLite for: development/testing, read-heavy workloads, single-server deployment, side projects/prototypes, or simplicity priority. Use MySQL for: existing MySQL infrastructure, team MySQL expertise, simple queries, or AWS RDS MySQL preference.
rule.rationale: PostgreSQL provides advanced features and strong Django integration ideal for production. SQLite offers development simplicity and zero-configuration deployment. MySQL serves legacy infrastructure. Choose based on production requirements, team expertise, and operational constraints.

rule.statement: Use PaaS (Render, Fly.io, Railway) for: simplicity, managed infrastructure, small teams, and rapid iteration. Use VPS (DigitalOcean, Hetzner) for: full control, cost optimization at scale, custom infrastructure, or team DevOps experience. Use Kubernetes for: horizontal scaling, zero-downtime deployments, high-availability, or microservices. Use Serverless for: highly variable traffic, cost optimization, stateless applications, or event-driven architecture.
rule.rationale: PaaS trades control for simplicity and speed. VPS provides control and cost efficiency. Kubernetes handles scaling but adds complexity. Serverless optimizes for cost and scaling. Choose based on team expertise, scaling requirements, and operational overhead tolerance.

rule.statement: Use this decision tree: (1) Changes database? → services/. (2) External API call? → services/ or integrations/. (3) Pure function? → utils/. (4) HTTP handling? → views/. (5) Data structure? → models/. (6) Input validation? → serializers/. (7) Background task? → tasks/. (8) Reusable? → core/ or common/.
rule.rationale: This hierarchy enforces testability (pure functions in utils), side effects isolation (services), and dependency direction (views depend on services, not vice versa). Correct dependencies prevent circular imports and improve code reusability.

rule.statement: Before adding a package, verify: (1) Maintenance — commits within 6 months, (2) Compatibility — Django 5.x + Python 3.11+ support, (3) Community — GitHub stars, PyPI downloads, (4) Documentation — clear and complete, (5) Test coverage — CI passing, good coverage, (6) Security — no known vulnerabilities, (7) License — compatible with your project, (8) Necessity — cannot be done with Django built-ins.
rule.rationale: Unmaintained, incompatible, or poorly documented packages create maintenance debt. Verify these signals before adding the dependency. Package Risk Assessment: Low (greater than 5k stars, active, documented), Medium (1-5k stars, maintained), High (less than 1k stars, no recent commits), Critical (abandoned, security issues).
